NAME="page_ds1243_1_53"> </A> </FONT></P>

<!-- TOC_END -->

<P><FONT SIZE=2><A
NAME="ds1243_item_7._management_s_discussio__ite03649"> </A>
<A NAME="toc_ds1243_1"> </A>
<BR></FONT><FONT SIZE=2><B>ITEM 7. MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS.    <BR>  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B><I>Overview  </I></B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We are one of the largest hotel/casino operators in the United States. Our operations consist of wholly owned resorts in Nevada and Mississippi, as well as
jointly owned resorts in Nevada, Illinois and Michigan. Our resorts cater to a wide variety of customers, and we strive to provide the best value in each of the markets where we compete. We generate
half of our net revenues from gaming activities, and nearly one-quarter from room sales. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our
core market is Las Vegas, where our properties generate approximately two-thirds of our operating income. Mandalay Bay has become the best performer among our properties
in this market. As our flagship operation, Mandalay Bay possesses amenities that appeal to higher-income customers. Strong demand from this segment of our customer base has permitted us greater
pricing leverage, which has helped to drive results at this property. With the addition of the convention center, all-suites hotel tower and retail center (each discussed more fully under
"Financial Position and Capital Resources&#151;New Projects" below), Mandalay Bay should continue to be the leading driver of future growth for our company. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our
operating results are highly dependent on the volume of customers visiting and staying at our resorts. This is particularly evident in our two principal revenue
centers&#151;the casino and the hotel. The volume of casino activity is measured by "drop," which refers to amounts wagered by our customers. The amount of drop which we keep and which is
recognized as casino revenue is referred to as our "win" or "hold." In our hotel business, a key metric is revenue per available room, or "REVPAR." REVPAR reflects both occupancy levels and room
rates, each of which is impacted by customer demand, among other factors. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><B><I>Critical Accounting Policies  </I></B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The consolidated financial statements included in this report were prepared in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States.
Certain accounting policies require us to apply significant judgment in defining the appropriate assumptions for calculating financial estimates. By their nature, these judgments are subject to an
inherent degree of uncertainty. These judgments are based on our historical experience, terms of existing contracts, observance of trends in the gaming industry and information available from other
outside sources. There can be no assurance that actual results will not differ from these estimates. The following is a summary of our critical accounting policies. See also the summary of significant
accounting policies included in Note&nbsp;1 of the Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements included in Item&nbsp;8 of this report. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Property and Equipment  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We have significant capital invested in our property and equipment, which represents almost 75% of our total assets. We utilize our judgement in various ways
including (i)determining whether an expenditure is considered a maintenance expense or a capital asset; (ii)&nbsp;determining the estimated useful lives of assets; (iii)&nbsp;determining the
salvage values to be assigned to assets; and (iv)&nbsp;determining if or when an asset has been impaired. The accuracy of these estimates affects the amount of depreciation expense recognized in our
income statement, whether we have a gain or loss on the disposal of an asset and whether or not we record an impairment loss related to an asset. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We
review the carrying value of our property and equipment whenever events and circumstances indicate that the carrying value of an asset may not be recoverable from the estimated future
cash flows expected to result from its use and eventual disposition. The factors we consider in performing this assessment include current operating results, trends and prospects, as well as the
effect of obsolescence, </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>53</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=1,SEQ=53,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=14126,FOLIO='53',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DS1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_ds1243_1_54"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
demand, competition and other economic factors. In estimating expected future cash flows for determining whether an asset is impaired, assets are grouped at the operating unit level, which for most
of our assets is the individual casino. If the sum of the expected future cash flows (undiscounted and without interest charges) is less than the carrying amount, we recognize an impairment loss. The
impairment loss recognized is the amount by which the carrying amount exceeds the fair value. The ability to accurately predict future cash flows may impact the determination of fair value, and hence,
the amount of the impairment loss. Our assessment of cash flows represents our best estimate as of the time of the impairment review and is consistent with our internal planning. Based upon our most
recent review of the carrying values of our property and equipment, we do not believe that there are additional impairments that have occurred or are likely to occur in the near term. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We have $38.3&nbsp;million in goodwill and $93.4&nbsp;million in intangible development costs on our consolidated balance sheet at January&nbsp;31, 2003.
Effective February&nbsp;1, 2002, we adopted Statement of Financial Accounting Standards No.&nbsp;142, "Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets" ("SFAS&nbsp;142"), which requires an annual review of
goodwill and other indefinite-life intangible assets for impairment. We completed our initial assessment of these assets, pursuant to which we recorded an impairment charge of
$1.9&nbsp;million (reflected as a cumulative effect of a change in accounting principle) in the first quarter of fiscal 2003. We also completed our annual assessment for impairment in the fourth
quarter of fiscal 2003, pursuant to which we recorded an additional impairment charge of $5.4&nbsp;million. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The
annual evaluation of goodwill and other indefinite-life intangible assets requires the use of estimates about future operating results of each reporting unit to determine
their estimated fair value. Changes in forecasted operations can materially affect these estimates. Once an impairment of goodwill or other intangible assets has been recorded, it cannot be reversed.
Based upon our most recent review of goodwill and another intangible asset, we do not believe that there are additional impairments that have occurred or are likely to occur in the near term. The
remaining goodwill relates entirely to our acquisition of Slots-A-Fun and our investment in MotorCity Casino in Detroit. The carrying value of goodwill related to these
investments, relative to the market value of the other assets, is small and furthermore, operating results at these properties have historically been strong and stable. Consequently, in our opinion,
it is unlikely that there will be additional goodwill impairment in the near term. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Players' Club Program  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our players' club program offers incentives to customers who gamble at our casinos. Customers earn points, redeemable for cash and complimentaries, based upon
their level of gambling. These points accumulate until such time as the customer elects to redeem them, up to a maximum of 18&nbsp;months at which time the points expire. We accrue expense related
to this program as the points are earned based upon historical redemption percentages and, in the case of complimentaries, the estimated cost of the complimentaries to be provided. The actual amount
of expense can differ from this estimate to the extent that actual redemptions differ from historical patterns. For example, to the extent players increase their redemption activity above historical
levels, the actual expense we incur related to our players' club could be significantly higher. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Bad Debt Reserves  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We reserve an estimated amount for receivables that may not be collected. These bad debt reserves are estimated using a combination of specific reserves and
various percentages applied to aged receivables based upon our judgment. We consider historical collection rates along with customer relationships in determining specific reserves. At
January&nbsp;31, 2003, we had $27.3&nbsp;million in our bad debt reserve, representing approximately 33% of the related receivables. To the extent world events </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>54</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=2,SEQ=54,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=474206,FOLIO='54',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DS1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_ds1243_1_55"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
such as economic downturns, war or further terrorist attacks impact the ability of our customers to pay us, our reserves could be inadequate. However, the majority of our casino receivables relate to
domestic play. Consequently, we believe our casino receivables are less exposed to the impact of some of these events. Notwithstanding, we believe our current reserve is appropriate and reasonable
based upon our experience. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Self-Insurance Accruals  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We are self-insured, up to certain limits, for costs associated with workers' compensation and employee medical coverage. Insurance claims and
reserves include accruals of estimated settlements for known claims, as well as accruals of estimates for claims incurred but not yet reported. At January&nbsp;31, 2003, we had total
self-insurance accruals reflected on our balance sheet of $8.0&nbsp;million. In estimating these accruals, we consider historical loss experience and make judgments about the expected
levels of costs per claim. We believe our estimates of future liability are reasonable based upon our methodology; however, changes in health care costs, accident frequency and severity and other
factors could materially affect the estimate for these liabilities. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Income Taxes  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We are subject to income taxes in the United States and in several states in which we operate. We account for income taxes according to Statement of Financial
Accounting Standards No.&nbsp;109, "Accounting for Income Taxes" ("SFAS&nbsp;109"). SFAS&nbsp;109 requires the recognition of deferred tax assets, net of applicable reserves, related to net
operating loss carryforwards and certain temporary differences. The standard requires recognition of a future tax benefit to the extent that realization of such benefit is more likely than not.
Otherwise, a valuation allowance is applied. At January&nbsp;31, 2003, we had $37.7&nbsp;million of deferred tax assets and $248.8&nbsp;million of deferred tax liabilities. We believe that our
deferred tax assets are fully realizable because of the future reversal of existing taxable temporary differences and future projected taxable income. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our
income tax returns are subject to examination by the Internal Revenue Service and other tax authorities. We regularly assess the potential outcomes of these examinations in
determining the adequacy of our provision for income taxes and our income tax liabilities. Inherent in our determination of any necessary reserves are assumptions based on past experiences and
judgments about potential actions by taxing authorities. Our estimate of the potential outcome for any uncertain tax issues is highly judgmental. We believe we have adequately provided for any
reasonable and foreseeable outcome related to uncertain tax matters. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;When
actual results of tax examinations differ from our estimates, we adjust the income tax provision in the period in which the examination issues are settled. In August&nbsp;2002, we
settled the IRS audits of
our fiscal 1992 through fiscal 1995 tax returns, which did not result in a material impact on our results of operations or financial position. The tax returns for years after fiscal 1995 are still
under examination or are subject to possible future examination. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Contingencies  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We are involved in various legal proceedings, as more fully discussed in Note&nbsp;17 of the Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements in Item&nbsp;8 of this
report. Due to their nature, such legal proceedings involve inherent uncertainties including, but not limited to, court rulings, negotiations between affected parties and governmental intervention.
Management assesses the probability of loss for such contingencies and accrues a liability and/or discloses the relevant circumstances, as appropriate. We believe that any liability to the company
that may arise as a result of currently pending legal proceedings will not have a material adverse effect on our financial condition, taken as a whole. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>55</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=3,SEQ=55,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=826224,FOLIO='55',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DS1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<!-- THIS IS THE END OF A COMPOSITION COMPONENT -->

<P><FONT SIZE=2><A
NAME="page_du1243_1_56"> </A> </FONT> <FONT SIZE=2><B><I>Fiscal 2003 Compared with Fiscal 2002  </I></B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>SIGNIFICANT FACTORS AFFECTING RESULTS OF OPERATIONS IN FISCAL 2003  </B></FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>September&nbsp;11, 2001  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We believe that the terrorist attacks of September&nbsp;11, 2001 continued to affect our operating results in fiscal 2003. Our Las Vegas properties are
particularly dependent on air travel for a substantial portion of their customers. While passenger counts in fiscal 2003 at McCarran International Airport, as well as overall Las Vegas visitor volume,
rebounded from the depressed levels immediately following September&nbsp;11, 2001, they remained below the counts recorded for fiscal 2001 (the last fiscal year unaffected by the attacks). As a
result, fewer customers visited or stayed at our Las Vegas properties, and this led to a drop in revenues at most of those properties in fiscal 2003. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our
properties in our other markets were not as vulnerable to the aftermath of September&nbsp;11, 2001, and, consequently, their operations do not appear to have been significantly
impacted. We cannot predict the extent to which the events of September&nbsp;11, 2001 will continue to directly or indirectly impact our operating results, nor can we predict the extent to which the
war with Iraq, future security alerts or additional terrorist attacks may interfere with our operations. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Economic Recession  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Historically, there has not been a high correlation between economic conditions and our operating results. This has been true with respect to the overall U.S.
economy and also the regional economies from which we derive a sizeable portion of our customers (e.g., California). However, we believe the
broad economic recession in the country last year did have a significant effect on our results for fiscal 2003. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Expansion of Native American Gaming  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On March&nbsp;7, 2000, California voters approved Proposition 1A, which amended the California constitution and legalized "Nevada-style" gaming on Native
American reservations. Since then, a significant expansion of Native American gaming operations has occurred in California and is continuing. This trend has caused generally lower operating results at
our properties in the secondary markets of Reno, Laughlin and Jean, Nevada. While most existing Native American gaming facilities in California are modest compared to our Nevada casinos, numerous
Native American tribes have announced they are developing, or are considering establishing, large-scale hotel and gaming facilities in California. Numerous other tribes are at various stages of
planning new or expanded facilities. The continued growth of Native American gaming establishments in California (as well as elsewhere in the country) could have a material adverse effect on our
operations. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>RESULTS OF OPERATIONS  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Earnings per Share  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2003, we reported net income of $115.6&nbsp;million, or $1.65 per diluted share, versus $53.0&nbsp;million, or $.71 per
diluted share, for the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2002. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The
increase in earnings for fiscal 2003 was due primarily to a $71.0&nbsp;million decrease in depreciation and amortization expense, which was partially offset by a related
$16.9&nbsp;million increase in operating lease rent (see "Depreciation and Amortization" below). Earnings in fiscal 2003 also benefitted from a $14.2&nbsp;million decrease in interest expense; an
overall reduction in write-offs related to asset impairment, goodwill impairment and intangible assets; and a decrease in the average number of diluted shares outstanding. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>56</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=1,SEQ=56,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=558121,FOLIO='56',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DU1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_du1243_1_57"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Earnings
for fiscal 2003 reflected strong results at Mandalay Bay and MotorCity Casino. At Mandalay Bay, operating income surged 92%, on the strength of cost-cutting measures
implemented after September&nbsp;11, 2001, lower depreciation and amortization expense and a decline in entertainment
expense due to the July&nbsp;2002 closing of the "Storm" production in the Mandalay showroom. Mandalay Bay also benefitted from the January&nbsp;3, 2003 opening of the new convention center. At
MotorCity Casino, operating income rose 27% (despite the intangible write-off discussed below), reflecting higher slot machine revenues at that property. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Other
factors affecting our fiscal 2003 and 2002 results included the following: </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Preopening expenses.</I></FONT><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We recorded preopening expenses of $4.6&nbsp;million for fiscal 2003 and $2.2&nbsp;million for
fiscal 2002. In both years, these expenses related primarily to the new convention center at Mandalay Bay that opened in January&nbsp;2003. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Adjustments to carrying values.</I></FONT><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At the end of each quarterly reporting period, we adjust the carrying value of investments
associated with our Supplemental Executive Retirement Plan ("SERP"), a defined benefit plan for senior executives, to reflect their market value. These noncash adjustments (reflected in the "Interest,
dividends and other income" caption) amounted to losses of $2.9&nbsp;million and $2.1&nbsp;million for fiscal 2003 and 2002, respectively. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Write-off of intangibles.</I></FONT><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Results for fiscal 2003 reflect the write-off of $13.0&nbsp;million of
intangible costs associated with MotorCity Casino in Detroit. These intangible costs represent amounts paid by Mandalay to its partner, Atwater Casino Group, in exchange for a so-called
preference (applicable to predecessors of Atwater Casino Group) to develop a casino in Detroit. In early 2002, preferences of this nature were declared unconstitutional by a federal appeals court. On
August&nbsp;2, 2002, MotorCity signed a Revised Development Agreement with the City of Detroit which provided for the development of an expanded permanent casino on the site of the current facility.
As a result of these events, it was determined that the intangible preference had no value and should be written off. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Goodwill impairment.</I></FONT><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Statement of Financial Accounting Standards No.&nbsp;142, "Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets"
("SFAS&nbsp;142"), became effective for Mandalay on February&nbsp;1, 2002. Under SFAS&nbsp;142, goodwill is no longer amortized, but must be reviewed at least annually for impairment. In
connection with our implementation of SFAS&nbsp;142, we completed an analysis of the goodwill arising from our prior acquisitions and recorded a write-off of $1.9&nbsp;million,
representing the unamortized goodwill associated with the June&nbsp;1, 1995 acquisition of the Railroad Pass Hotel and Casino in Henderson, Nevada. This write-off was reflected as a
cumulative effect of a change in accounting principle in the quarter ended April&nbsp;30, 2002. Pursuant to our subsequent annual review for goodwill impairment, we recorded a write-off
of $5.4&nbsp;million that was reflected as an impairment loss in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2003. This write-off represented the unamortized goodwill associated with the
February&nbsp;1, 1983 acquisition of the Edgewater Hotel and Casino in Laughlin, Nevada. It was necessitated by a decline in income from operations at that property, as well as lower future
expectations attributable to the impact on the Laughlin market of increased competition from Native American casinos. The effects of the above write-offs were offset by the elimination of
goodwill amortization in fiscal 2003. Goodwill amortization in fiscal 2002 had amounted to $11.8&nbsp;million. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Other impairment.</I></FONT><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In fiscal 2002, we recorded an impairment loss of $52.0&nbsp;million related to the
write-down of the carrying value of our two Jean properties, Gold Strike and Nevada Landing. This write-down reflected the downturn in these properties' operating results due
to the continued expansion of Native American casinos in California. The write-down was made in accordance with Statement of Financial Accounting Standards No.&nbsp;121, "Accounting for
the Impairment of Long-Lived Assets and for Long-Lived Assets to be Disposed Of." Of the $52.0&nbsp;million write-down, $17.9&nbsp;million represented goodwill. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>57</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=2,SEQ=57,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=638055,FOLIO='57',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DU1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_du1243_1_58"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Revenues  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Revenues increased $5.6&nbsp;million, or less than 1%, for the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2003. The increase was due to a $35.9&nbsp;million, or 10%,
increase in revenues at MotorCity Casino in Detroit, Michigan. This increase was partially offset by lower revenues at our Las Vegas Strip properties stemming from the continued effect of
September&nbsp;11, 2001 and the recession. Revenues at these properties were down $13.8&nbsp;million, or 1%. Revenues also declined at our other Nevada properties, mainly because of expanded
Native American gaming in California. In Reno, revenues decreased $9.2&nbsp;million, or 8%; in Laughlin they were down $4.1&nbsp;million, or 3%; and in Jean they were down $4.4&nbsp;million, or
5%. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Casino Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Casino revenues rose $3.5&nbsp;million, or less than 1%, in fiscal 2003. Casino revenues declined at most of our major wholly owned properties due to the impact
of the significant factors discussed previously (the exceptions were Luxor in Las Vegas and Gold Strike in Tunica County, Mississippi, which recorded slight increases). These declines were offset by a
$35.9&nbsp;million, or 10%, increase in casino revenues at MotorCity Casino. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Room Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Room revenues fell $11.3&nbsp;million, or 2%, in fiscal 2003. Despite improving steadily throughout fiscal 2003, REVPAR at our Las Vegas properties decreased 2%
from fiscal 2002 due mainly to the lingering effects of the September&nbsp;11, 2001 attacks and the recession. The following table compares average room rates, occupancy and REVPAR at our major
wholly owned properties: </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Food and Beverage Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Food and beverage revenues increased $3.8&nbsp;million, or 1%, in fiscal 2003. The increase was mainly due to the expansion of Mandalay Bay's convention
business following the opening of the new convention center on January&nbsp;3, 2003. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Other Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Other revenues come principally from entertainment, amusements and retail stores. Other revenues rose $1.7&nbsp;million, or 1%, in fiscal 2003. The continued
success of Blue Man Group at Luxor largely offset the impact from the closing of "Storm" at Mandalay Bay. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>58</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=3,SEQ=58,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=1006002,FOLIO='58',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DU1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_du1243_1_59"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Income from Operations  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2003, income from operations rose $101.2&nbsp;million, or 29%, from the previous year. The composite operating margin in
fiscal 2003 was 19.2% versus 14.9% in fiscal 2002. Income from operations for fiscal 2003 benefitted from the reduction of depreciation and amortization expense and from cost-cutting
measures undertaken at all of our properties following September&nbsp;11, 2001. These benefits were partially offset by the $13.0&nbsp;million write-off of intangible assets at
MotorCity Casino and the $5.4&nbsp;million goodwill write-off at Edgewater discussed earlier. Meanwhile, income from operations in fiscal 2002 was negatively affected by the previously
discussed impairment loss of $52.0&nbsp;million related to our Jean, Nevada properties. The table below summarizes our operating results by property and is followed by a discussion of operating
results by market. </FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE ALIGN="LEFT" WIDTH="120">
<DL compact>
<DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(1)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Earnings
before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization ("EBITDA"). EBITDA is included because, as in other entertainment industries, many investors consider it an important
measure of property performance, since it factors out the impact of depreciation and amortization, the principal noncash expenses affecting income from operations. EBITDA is not an accepted measure of
performance under GAAP and should not be considered an alternative to GAAP measures of performance.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(2)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Includes
Circus Circus&#151;Las Vegas and Slots-A-Fun.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(3)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Includes
Gold Strike, Nevada Landing and Railroad Pass.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(4)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>MotorCity
Casino is 53.5%-owned and its operations are consolidated for financial reporting purposes.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(5)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Includes
Monte Carlo, Grand Victoria and Silver Legacy, each of which is 50%-owned. </FONT></DD></DL>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Las Vegas  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our Las Vegas properties (including our 50% share of Monte Carlo) posted an overall increase in income from operations of $42.4&nbsp;million, or 17%, for fiscal
2003. At Mandalay Bay, income from operations rose $38.2&nbsp;million, or 92%, despite flat revenues. The main contributors to the increase at </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>59</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=4,SEQ=59,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=737966,FOLIO='59',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DU1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_du1243_1_60"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
Mandalay Bay were the cost-cutting measures implemented after September&nbsp;11, 2001, a combined $10.1&nbsp;million reduction in depreciation expense and operating lease rent (see
the discussions under "Depreciation and Amortization" and "Operating Lease Rent") and a decrease in entertainment expense stemming from the July&nbsp;2002 closing of the "Storm" production in the
Mandalay showroom. The preproduction costs associated with this show were fully expensed in fiscal 2002 in anticipation of the closing. "Storm" was replaced by the Broadway hit "Mamma Mia!", which
opened February&nbsp;3, 2003. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;While
cost-cutting measures also benefitted our other Las Vegas properties, results were mixed. Income from operations at Luxor in fiscal 2003 was up $4.7&nbsp;million, or
6%, while the contribution from Monte Carlo increased by $2.4&nbsp;million, or 8%, for the year. However, at Excalibur, income from operations was down $2.7&nbsp;million, or 4%, and at Circus
Circus it declined $0.3&nbsp;million, or 1%. These latter two properties cater primarily to the middle-income market segment, which appears to have been more impacted by the significant factors
discussed previously. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Reno  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Income from operations at our Reno properties (including our 50% share of Silver Legacy) was down $3.3&nbsp;million, or 11%, in fiscal 2003. Expanded Native
American gaming in California and the northwestern U.S. contributed to the decline, as did the economic recession. The absence from Reno of the American Bowling Congress National Championship, which
was held there in fiscal 2002, also affected our results. However, in fiscal 2004, the women's national bowling tournament will be held in Reno, which is scheduled to host the men's or women's
national bowling tournament in two out of every three years through fiscal 2010. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Laughlin  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our two Laughlin properties, Colorado Belle and Edgewater, posted a combined decrease in income from operations of $5.2&nbsp;million, or 50%, for fiscal 2003.
The decline was due to the write-off of $5.4&nbsp;million of goodwill associated with Edgewater, as discussed previously. Furthermore, like the Reno market, Laughlin is facing increased
competition from Native American casinos in its primary feeder markets in Arizona and southern California. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Other Markets  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In Detroit, Michigan, MotorCity Casino generated an increase in income from operations of $19.6&nbsp;million, or 27%, despite the $13.0&nbsp;million
write-off of an intangible asset discussed previously. Lower depreciation expense (down $14.2&nbsp;million) was the principal factor in this increase (see discussion under "Depreciation
and Amortization"), though a 15% increase in slot revenues was also a contributing factor. See "Financial Position and Capital Resources&#151;New Projects" for additional details regarding our
Detroit operation. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In
Tunica County, Mississippi, income from operations at Gold Strike increased $3.0&nbsp;million, or 27%, during fiscal 2003. A 2% rise in casino revenue, stemming from expanded
marketing efforts, was largely responsible for the increase, along with cost-cutting measures. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The
contribution to income from operations made by Grand Victoria (our 50%-owned riverboat casino in Elgin, Illinois) decreased $9.7&nbsp;million, or 17%, in fiscal 2003. The decrease
was due primarily to a tax increase on casinos that took effect July&nbsp;1, 2002 and included a top-end rate of 50% on gaming revenues exceeding $200&nbsp;million. This tax increase
reduced the contribution from Grand Victoria by $16.7 million for the year. Had the new legislation been in effect for all of fiscal 2003, the contribution from Grand Victoria would have been lower by
$20.3&nbsp;million. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>60</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=5,SEQ=60,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=316482,FOLIO='60',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DU1243A.;11',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<BR>
<!-- THIS IS THE END OF A COMPOSITION COMPONENT -->

<P><FONT SIZE=2><A
NAME="page_dw1243_1_61"> </A> </FONT> <FONT SIZE=2><I>Depreciation and Amortization  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For fiscal 2003, depreciation and amortization expense was $145.0&nbsp;million versus $216.0&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002. The $71.0&nbsp;million decrease
was due to several factors, including new operating leases entered into in December&nbsp;2001. Under these new leases, we sold and leased back $130.5&nbsp;million of equipment, thus replacing
depreciation expense and interest expense with operating lease rent. See the discussion under "Financial Position and Capital Resources&#151;Off Balance Sheet Arrangements" for more details
regarding these leases. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Depreciation
and amortization was also lower due to a reduction of depreciation at MotorCity Casino. Based upon a Revised Development Agreement entered into with the City of Detroit on
August&nbsp;2, 2002 (discussed more fully under "Financial Position and Capital Resources&#151;New Projects"), depreciation expense related to the MotorCity Casino facility was reduced
prospectively. Previously, the cost of the temporary facility was being depreciated over its contractual operating term of four years, with the annual depreciation expense approximating
$40&nbsp;million. Under the Revised Development Agreement, the temporary facility ceases to be temporary and will be expanded as a permanent resort (as opposed to the permanent resort being
developed on a separate site, as was the requirement under the original development agreement). Consequently, the remaining book value of the temporary facility is being depreciated over its remaining
expected life. This will result in estimated depreciation expense of $8-$10&nbsp;million in fiscal 2004. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Finally,
depreciation and amortization expense also declined due to the previously discussed elimination of goodwill amortization pursuant to SFAS&nbsp;142. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Operating Lease Rent  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Operating lease rent for fiscal 2003 increased to $49.1&nbsp;million from $32.2&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002. The increase was due to the new operating leases
we signed in December&nbsp;2001. The higher rent expense we incurred as a result of those leases, however, was partially offset by a reduction in rent expense related to the June&nbsp;2002 renewal
of the previously existing $200&nbsp;million lease facility. See the discussion under "Financial Position and Capital Resources&#151;Off Balance Sheet Arrangements" for additional details
regarding our operating lease agreements. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Interest Expense  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In fiscal 2003, interest expense (excluding interest expense of unconsolidated joint ventures and without reduction for capitalized interest) decreased
$2.1&nbsp;million. The decrease was primarily due to a combination of lower interest rates on short-term borrowings, new fixed to floating interest rate swap agreements entered into in
June and August&nbsp;2002 and lower average borrowings. See "Financial Position and Capital Resources&#151;Financing Activities" for a discussion of the new swaps. This reduction of interest
expense was partially offset by the issuance of $300&nbsp;million principal amount of 9<SUP>3</SUP>/<SMALL>8</SMALL>% Senior Subordinated Notes in December&nbsp;2001. The net proceeds from this issuance were
used to pay down lower-cost borrowings outstanding under our bank facility. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On
March&nbsp;5, 2002, Silver Legacy issued $160&nbsp;million of 10<SUP>1</SUP>/<SMALL>8</SMALL>% Mortgage Notes due 2012. At the same time, Silver Legacy entered into a new senior secured
credit facility, comprised of a $20&nbsp;million revolving credit facility and a $20&nbsp;million amortizing term loan. The net proceeds of the notes, combined with draws under the new credit
facility, were used to repay the prior credit facility ($152.3&nbsp;million) and to fund a $30&nbsp;million distribution to the Silver Legacy partners ($20&nbsp;million of which was distributed
to Mandalay). </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At
January&nbsp;31, 2003, long-term debt (including current portion) stood at $2.78&nbsp;billion compared to $2.52&nbsp;billion at January&nbsp;31, 2002. The fiscal
2003 total includes $20.0&nbsp;million of debt related to MotorCity Casino, while the fiscal 2002 total includes $64.0&nbsp;million of debt related to MotorCity. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>61</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=1,SEQ=61,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=565336,FOLIO='61',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DW1243A.;8',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_dw1243_1_62"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
Capitalized interest was $13.2&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003 compared to $1.0&nbsp;million in the previous year. Capitalized interest in both years related primarily to the new convention center at
Mandalay Bay. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Income Taxes  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The effective tax rates for fiscal 2003 and fiscal 2002 were 40.2% and 43.0%. These rates reflect the corporate statutory rate of 35% plus the effect of various
nondeductible expenses, primarily the amortization and write-off of goodwill. The higher rate in fiscal 2002 was due to the impairment loss of $52.0&nbsp;million, $17.9&nbsp;million of
which represented the write-off of goodwill, which is not deductible for tax purposes. For fiscal 2004, we estimate our effective tax rate will be 36%-37%. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B><I>Fiscal 2002 Compared with Fiscal 2001  </I></B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>SIGNIFICANT FACTORS AFFECTING RESULTS OF OPERATIONS IN FISCAL 2002  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>September&nbsp;11, 2001  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The terrorist attacks of September&nbsp;11, 2001 had a pronounced effect on our operating results for the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2002. This effect was
felt primarily at our Las Vegas properties, which generate approximately two-thirds of our operating income. Our Las Vegas properties, with almost 19,000 hotel rooms, are dependent on air
travel for a significant portion of their customers. This is particularly true at our more upscale resorts, Mandalay Bay and Luxor, where over 50% of the hotel customers arrive via plane. As a result
of the terrorist attacks, air travel plummeted nationally. Passenger counts at McCarran International Airport in Las Vegas fell almost 20% over the last four months of 2001, leading to an overall 4.5%
decline for the year. The number of visitors to Las Vegas declined 2.3% in 2001, despite being up 1.0% for the year-to-date period prior to September&nbsp;11. The decreases
in air traffic and visitor volume caused declines in the number of customers staying at and visiting our Las Vegas properties. This situation was particularly acute immediately following the attacks,
when occupancy levels at our Las Vegas properties fell to the mid-60% range, compared to a normal occupancy above 90%. These factors led to declines in revenues and operating income at our
Las Vegas properties during the fiscal year. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our
operations in Reno, Laughlin and Jean, Nevada, were not as vulnerable to the aftermath of September&nbsp;11. These markets are essentially drive-in markets. While air
traffic fell sharply following the attacks, automobile traffic to Laughlin and Jean actually rose, while traffic to Reno showed a modest drop. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our
joint venture properties in Elgin, Illinois, and Detroit, Michigan, did not reflect any adverse impact from the terrorist attacks, as their revenues and operating income after
September&nbsp;11 were higher than they had been in the previous year. These properties serve essentially local markets (a majority of our customers live within a relatively short distance of the
properties) and so were minimally affected by travel concerns in the aftermath of the attacks. Detroit also benefitted from the temporary closure of the U.S./Canadian border immediately following the
terrorist attacks (and from subsequently increased security), which impeded access from the U.S. to a competing property in Windsor, Ontario. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In
response to the terrorist attacks, we took several steps to minimize their effect on our operations and our financial position. We temporarily (until February&nbsp;2002) suspended
construction of our new convention center at Mandalay Bay and we delayed or suspended all other discretionary capital spending. Additionally, at the beginning of October&nbsp;2001, we laid off
approximately 4,500 employees out of a total workforce of over 30,000 employees (including those at our joint ventures). Most of the layoffs were at our Las Vegas properties. We also froze wage levels
for nonunion personnel and reduced compensation for many management-level employees. Finally, we made several financing decisions designed to enhance our liquidity, such as amending our credit
facilities to ease financial </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>62</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=2,SEQ=62,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=247720,FOLIO='62',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DW1243A.;8',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_dw1243_1_63"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
covenants, issuing additional senior subordinated notes and entering into additional operating lease arrangements. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Economic Recession  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Historically, there has not been a high correlation between economic conditions and our operating results. This has been true with respect to the overall U.S.
economy and also the regional economies from which we derive a substantial portion of our customers (e.g., California). However, we believe the broad economic recession that affected the country
throughout most of 2001 and into 2002, and that was exacerbated by the terrorist attacks on September&nbsp;11, contributed to the downturn in our results for fiscal 2002. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Expansion of Native American Gaming  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On March&nbsp;7, 2000, California voters approved Proposition 1A, which amended the California constitution and legalized "Nevada-style" gaming on Native
American reservations. The passage of this amendment allowed a significant expansion of Native American gaming operations to occur in the latter part of fiscal 2001 and the early part of fiscal 2002.
The result was a decline in operating results at our properties in the secondary markets of Reno, Laughlin and Jean, Nevada. The impact of expanded Native American gaming was particularly significant
at Gold Strike and Nevada Landing in Jean, and was the principal factor behind the impairment loss that was recognized at those properties in fiscal 2002 (see discussion under "Results of
Operations"). </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>RESULTS OF OPERATIONS  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Earnings per Share  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2002, we reported net income of $53.0&nbsp;million, or $.71 per diluted share, versus $119.7&nbsp;million, or $1.50 per
diluted share, for the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2001. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The
decrease in earnings per share was attributable primarily to an impairment loss of $52.0&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002 related to the write-down of the carrying value of
our two Jean properties, Gold Strike and
Nevada Landing. This write-down reflected the downturn in operating results at these properties over the previous few years due to the continued expansion of Native American casinos in
California. The write-down was made in accordance with Statement of Financial Accounting Standards No.&nbsp;121, "Accounting for the Impairment of Long-Lived Assets and for
Long-Lived Assets to Be Disposed Of" ("SFAS&nbsp;121"). Of the $52.0&nbsp;million write-down, $17.9&nbsp;million represented goodwill. Results for fiscal 2002 also
included preopening expenses of $2.2&nbsp;million related to the construction of a new convention center at Mandalay Bay. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Fiscal
2002 earnings were affected as well by the September&nbsp;11 attacks, the recession and higher utility costs. Utility costs rose $12.7&nbsp;million, or 28%, at our wholly
owned properties, with most of the increase attributable to our Las Vegas Strip properties. Utility costs were higher due to the spillover effect from the California energy crisis. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The
negative effect of all of the above factors on our earnings per share was partially offset by a lower number of average diluted shares outstanding (74.5&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002
versus 79.7&nbsp;million in fiscal 2001). The lower number was due to our purchase of shares. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Results
for fiscal 2001 included preopening expenses of $1.8&nbsp;million related to the Shark Reef at Mandalay Bay, which opened June&nbsp;20, 2000. Results also included
$3.6&nbsp;million in income related to reducing a liability assumed when the Mandalay Bay site was acquired in 1995. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>63</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=3,SEQ=63,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=94929,FOLIO='63',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DW1243A.;8',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_dw1243_1_64"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Revenues  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Revenues decreased $32.6&nbsp;million, or 1%, for the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2002. The decrease was due primarily to the effect of September&nbsp;11 on
our Las Vegas Strip properties, whose revenues were down $28.3&nbsp;million, or 2%. Revenues also declined at our other Nevada properties, mainly because of expanded Native American gaming in
California. In Reno, revenues decreased $5.2&nbsp;million, or 4%; in Laughlin they were down $11.5&nbsp;million, or 7%; and in Jean they were down $14.4&nbsp;million, or 17%. These declines were
partially offset by a $33.5&nbsp;million, or 10%, increase in revenues at MotorCity Casino in Detroit, Michigan. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Casino Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Casino revenues declined $19.9&nbsp;million, or 2%, in fiscal 2002. Casino revenues declined at all of our major wholly owned properties due to the impact of
the significant factors discussed previously. These
declines were partially offset by a $35.4&nbsp;million, or 11%, increase in casino revenues at MotorCity Casino. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Room Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Room revenues fell $29.8&nbsp;million, or 5%, in fiscal 2002. Our Las Vegas properties experienced strong growth in revenue per available room ("REVPAR")
through the first part of the year, mainly as a result of increases in room rates. However, following September&nbsp;11, occupancy rates declined dramatically and room rates were discounted in order
to stimulate demand. As a result, REVPAR fell noticeably in the last quarter of the year. The following table compares average room rates, occupancy and REVPAR at our major wholly owned properties: </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Food and Beverage Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Food and beverage revenues decreased $7.8&nbsp;million, or 2%, in fiscal 2002, with the decline attributable principally to the impact that September&nbsp;11
had on our Las Vegas Strip properties. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Other Revenues  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Other revenues rose $32.5&nbsp;million, or 11%, in fiscal 2002. Other revenues come principally from entertainment, amusements and retail stores. Most of the
increase was due to the success of Blue Man Group, the unique off-Broadway production which debuted in March&nbsp;2000 at Luxor. The April&nbsp;2001 opening of our new production,
"Storm," in the Mandalay Bay showroom also contributed to the increase, as did the Shark Reef at Mandalay Bay (which opened June&nbsp;2000). </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>64</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=4,SEQ=64,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=443584,FOLIO='64',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DW1243A.;8',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<BR>
<!-- THIS IS THE END OF A COMPOSITION COMPONENT -->

<P><FONT SIZE=2><A
NAME="page_dy1243_1_65"> </A> </FONT> <FONT SIZE=2><I>Income from Operations  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For the year ended January&nbsp;31, 2002, income from operations declined $80.5&nbsp;million, or 19%, from the previous year. The composite operating margin
in fiscal 2002 was 14.9% versus 18.1% in fiscal 2001. The previously discussed impairment loss of $52.0&nbsp;million recognized at our Jean properties was a principal cause of the declines in
operating income and operating margin. Our Las Vegas Strip properties also contributed to the declines, as the effects of September&nbsp;11 depressed results in that market, particularly in the
hotel department where our operating margins are typically the highest. The table below summarizes operating results by property and is followed by a discussion of operating results by market. </FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE ALIGN="LEFT" WIDTH="120">
<DL compact>
<DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(1)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Earnings
before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization ("EBITDA"). EBITDA is included because, as in other entertainment industries, many investors consider it an important
measure of property performance, since it factors out the impact of depreciation and amortization, the principal noncash expenses affecting operating income. EBITDA is not an accepted measure of
performance under GAAP and should not be considered an alternative to GAAP measures of performance.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(2)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Includes
Circus Circus&#151;Las Vegas and Slots-A-Fun.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(3)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Includes
Gold Strike, Nevada Landing and Railroad Pass.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(4)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>MotorCity
Casino is 53.5%-owned and its operations are consolidated for financial reporting purposes.
<BR><BR></FONT></DD><DT style='margin-bottom:-11pt;'><FONT SIZE=2>(5)</FONT></DT><DD><FONT SIZE=2>Includes
Monte Carlo, Grand Victoria and Silver Legacy, each of which is 50%-owned. </FONT></DD></DL>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Las Vegas  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our Las Vegas properties (including our 50% share of Monte Carlo) posted an overall decrease in operating income of $32.5&nbsp;million, or 11%, for fiscal 2002.
At Mandalay Bay, operating income rose $3.2&nbsp;million, or 8%, due primarily to increases in room rates that were achieved during the first part of the fiscal year, prior to the events of
September&nbsp;11. Because of the upscale clientele at this property, it </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>65</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=1,SEQ=65,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=910219,FOLIO='65',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DY1243A.;10',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_dy1243_1_66"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
possesses greater pricing leverage than our other properties. Operating income at our other Las Vegas properties declined for the year, tracking the lower revenues that followed September&nbsp;11.
At Excalibur operating income was down $11.4&nbsp;million, or 14%; at Luxor it was down $11.0&nbsp;million, or 13%; and at Circus Circus it was off by $7.9&nbsp;million, or 17%. The contribution
from Monte Carlo also declined, falling $5.4&nbsp;million, or 14%, for the year. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Reno  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Operating income from our Reno properties (including our 50% share of Silver Legacy) was down $5.5&nbsp;million, or 15%, in fiscal 2002, despite the presence in
Reno of the men's national bowling tournament. Operating results at our Reno properties have been adversely impacted by the expansion of Native American gaming in California and the northwestern U.S.
The events of September&nbsp;11 also contributed to the decline in operating income. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Laughlin  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Our two Laughlin properties, Colorado Belle and Edgewater, posted a combined decrease in operating income of $3.1&nbsp;million, or 23%, for fiscal 2002. While
REVPAR was flat, casino revenues decreased 8%. Like the Reno market, Laughlin is facing increased competition from Native American casinos in its primary feeder markets in Arizona and southern
California. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Other Markets  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In Detroit, Michigan, MotorCity Casino generated operating income of $72.2&nbsp;million, an increase of 46% over the prior year. Results at this property have
steadily improved since its December&nbsp;1999 opening. The property also benefitted from the temporary closure of the U.S./Canadian border immediately following the September&nbsp;11 attacks.
This measure, along with subsequently increased security, impeded access from the U.S. to a competing property in Windsor, Ontario. We believe MotorCity Casino has been able to attract a significant
number of customers who previously frequented the competing property in Windsor. See "Financial Position and Capital Resources&#151;New Projects" for additional details regarding our Detroit
operation. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In
Tunica County, Mississippi, operating income at Gold Strike decreased $8.4&nbsp;million, or 43%, during fiscal 2002. We believe the slow economy had a more pronounced effect on this
region, and especially on Memphis, Tennessee, which accounts for over half the property's customer base. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The
contribution to income from operations made by Grand Victoria (a 50%-owned riverboat casino in Elgin, Illinois) increased $5.0&nbsp;million, or 9%, for fiscal 2002. This market was
apparently not negatively impacted by the terrorist attacks on September&nbsp;11. It may have actually benefitted to the extent potential customers in this market were less willing to travel outside
the region. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Depreciation and Amortization  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For fiscal 2002, depreciation and amortization expense was $216.0&nbsp;million versus $218.0&nbsp;million in fiscal 2001. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Interest Expense  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In fiscal 2002, interest expense (excluding interest expense of unconsolidated joint ventures and without reduction for capitalized interest) increased
$0.8&nbsp;million over fiscal 2001. The increase was due to the issuance of $500&nbsp;million principal amount of 10<SUP>1</SUP>/<SMALL>4</SMALL>% Senior Subordinated Notes in July&nbsp;2000, the issuance
of $200&nbsp;million principal amount of 9<SUP>1</SUP>/<SMALL>2</SMALL>% Senior Notes in August&nbsp;2000, and the issuance of $300&nbsp;million principal amount of 9<SUP>3</SUP>/<SMALL>8</SMALL>% Senior Subordinated
Notes in December&nbsp;2001. The net proceeds from these offerings were used to pay down lower-cost borrowings outstanding under our bank facility. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>66</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=2,SEQ=66,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=896564,FOLIO='66',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DY1243A.;10',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_dy1243_1_67"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
The higher interest rates on the notes we issued were largely offset by lower average interest rates on our bank facility, as well as lower average borrowings outstanding. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At
January&nbsp;31, 2002, long-term debt (including current portion) stood at $2.52&nbsp;billion compared to $2.67&nbsp;billion at January&nbsp;31, 2001. The fiscal
2002 total included $64.0&nbsp;million of debt related to MotorCity Casino, while the fiscal 2001 total included $127.0&nbsp;million of debt related to MotorCity. Capitalized interest was
$1.0&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002 compared to $1.6&nbsp;million in the previous year. Capitalized interest in fiscal 2002 related primarily to the new convention center at Mandalay Bay. Capitalized
interest in the prior year was associated with the Shark Reef at Mandalay Bay. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Income Taxes  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The effective tax rates for fiscal 2002 and fiscal 2001 were 43.0% and 38.4%. These rates reflect the corporate statutory rate of 35% plus the effect of various
nondeductible expenses, primarily the amortization of goodwill. The higher rate in fiscal 2002 was due to the impairment loss of $52.0&nbsp;million. As previously discussed, $17.9&nbsp;million of
this loss represented the write-off of goodwill, which is not deductible for tax purposes. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>FINANCIAL POSITION AND CAPITAL RESOURCES  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Operating Activities  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For fiscal 2003, net cash provided by operating activities was $349.9&nbsp;million versus $358.2&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002 and $435.6&nbsp;million in
fiscal 2001. For fiscal 2003, net cash provided by operating activities decreased slightly, despite net income increasing $62.6&nbsp;million. The increase in net income was offset by the previously
discussed decrease in depreciation and amortization of $71.0&nbsp;million. Meanwhile, the decrease in fiscal 2002 was attributable to several factors, including a decrease in the deferred tax
liability, a smaller increase in interest payable, lower distributions from unconsolidated affiliates and lower net income stemming from September&nbsp;11. The decrease in the deferred tax liability
was due primarily to the write-down of our Jean properties, which is not deductible for tax purposes until the assets are actually disposed of. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mandalay
had cash and cash equivalents of $148.4&nbsp;million at January&nbsp;31, 2003, sufficient for normal daily operating requirements. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Investing Activities  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net cash used in investing activities was $370.3&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003 versus $159.9&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002 and $153.8&nbsp;million in fiscal
2001. Capital expenditures and the related increase or decrease in construction payable accounted for most of these amounts. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Capital
expenditures for fiscal 2003, which were funded primarily from cash flow, totaled $300.5&nbsp;million. Of this amount, $180.4&nbsp;million related to the construction of a
new convention center at Mandalay Bay that opened in January&nbsp;2003, $18.5&nbsp;million related to the construction of the new 1,122-suites tower at Mandalay Bay (see "New Projects"
for additional details), $15.4&nbsp;million related to the remodeling of the original suites at Mandalay Bay completed in fiscal 2003 and $8.2&nbsp;million related to the new retail center under
construction between Mandalay Bay and Luxor. For fiscal 2002, capital expenditures totaled $156.7&nbsp;million. Of this amount, $61.6&nbsp;million related to the construction of the new convention
center at Mandalay Bay. Another $8.4&nbsp;million related to the completion of the renovation of the pyramid rooms at Luxor. Capital expenditures also included $13.0&nbsp;million related to the
acquisition of land adjacent to our casino facility in Detroit. Capital expenditures for fiscal 2001 totaled $110.2&nbsp;million. Of this amount, $24.7&nbsp;million related to the Shark Reef at
Mandalay Bay, a saltwater aquarium attraction which opened June&nbsp;20, 2000, and $15.6&nbsp;million related to the renovation of the Luxor rooms. </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>67</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>
<!-- ZEQ.=3,SEQ=67,EFW="2109157",CP="MANDALAY RESORT GROUP",DN="1",CHK=178237,FOLIO='67',FILE='DISK039:[03WLA3.03WLA1243]DY1243A.;10',USER='CPETRER',CD=';1-MAY-2003;14:05' -->
<A NAME="page_dy1243_1_68"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We
estimate that capital expenditures in fiscal 2004 will be in the range of $300-$350&nbsp;million. The majority of these expenditures will relate to the completion of the
new all-suites tower at Mandalay Bay. These estimated capital expenditures also include maintenance capital spending, which consists of items necessary to maintain the operating condition
of our properties, such as new slot machines, carpeting, computers and similar equipment. Capital expenditures for fiscal 2004 will be funded primarily from cash flow, though we also have funds
available under our revolving credit facility. Actual capital expenditures for fiscal 2004 may differ significantly from the estimated range, particularly if we are able to proceed with the
development of an expanded permanent facility in Detroit. See "New Projects&#151;Detroit" for additional details. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><I>Financing Activities  </I></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For fiscal 2003, financing activities provided net cash of $62.9&nbsp;million. Net borrowings provided cash of $264.4&nbsp;million, while stock option
exercises provided an additional $44.3&nbsp;million. The majority of this cash was used to purchase 7.5&nbsp;million shares of our common stock at a cost of $220.9&nbsp;million. See "Share
Purchases" for further discussion of our share purchase activity. For fiscal 2002, financing activities used net cash of $198.4&nbsp;million, of which $125.9&nbsp;million was used for the purchase
of 5.2&nbsp;million shares of our common stock. Another $45.5&nbsp;million was used to fund interim settlements and interest payments under our equity forward agreements with Bank of America. See
the discussion under "Off Balance Sheet Arrangements" for more details regarding our equity forward agreements. For fiscal
2001, financing activities used net cash of $292.4&nbsp;million, most of which related to the purchase of 14.5&nbsp;million shares of our common stock at a cost of $247.1&nbsp;million. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Credit Facilities  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In August&nbsp;2001, we replaced our $1.8&nbsp;billion unsecured credit facility with three separate facilities that totaled $1.25&nbsp;billion. These
credit facilities included a $150&nbsp;million capital markets term loan, which we paid in full using a portion of the net proceeds we received from the issuance of $300&nbsp;million of Senior
Subordinated Notes in December&nbsp;2001. By paying off this facility, we reduced our borrowing capacity under the two remaining facilities to $1.1&nbsp;billion. Those two facilities, which are
for general corporate purposes, include a $250&nbsp;million term loan facility and an $850&nbsp;million revolving facility. The entire amount of the term loan facility was outstanding at
January&nbsp;31, 2003. Of the revolving facility, $410&nbsp;million was outstanding at January&nbsp;31, 2003. Each of our credit facilities is unsecured and provides for the payment of interest,
at our option, at either (i)&nbsp;a Eurodollar-based rate; or (ii)&nbsp;a rate equal to or an increment above the higher of (a)&nbsp;the Bank of America prime rate, or (b)&nbsp;the Federal
Reserve Board federal funds rate plus 50 basis points. At January&nbsp;31, 2003, the effective rate of interest on the indebtedness outstanding under our credit facilities was 3.2%. Each of our
credit facilities includes financial covenants regarding total debt and interest coverage, plus covenants that limit our ability to dispose of assets, make distributions on our capital stock, engage
in a merger, incur liens and engage in transactions with our affiliates. The entire principal amount then outstanding under our credit facilities becomes due and payable on August&nbsp;21, 2006,
unless the maturity date is extended with the consent of the lenders. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In
December&nbsp;2001, we amended the covenants under each of our credit facilities to provide for more liberal tests for total debt and interest coverage. These amendments were
obtained to address the impact of the events of September&nbsp;11, 2001. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In
February&nbsp;2003, we again amended the covenants under each of our credit facilities. These amendments modify the definition of "Adjusted EBITDA" with respect to our 53.5%
ownership of MotorCity Casino in Detroit, Michigan. As previously defined in our credit facilities, Adjusted EBITDA included only the cash distributions we actually received from MotorCity Casino.
Under the amended definition, Adjusted EBITDA will include our 53.5% share of the Adjusted EBITDA of MotorCity </FONT></P>

<P ALIGN="CENTER"><FONT SIZE=2>68</FONT></P>

<HR NOSHADE>

<A NAME="page_dy1243_1_69"> </A>
<BR>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>
Casino, whether or not distributed. These amendments also provide for a more liberal test for total debt coverage during the fiscal year ending January&nbsp;31, 2004. </FONT></P>


<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;As
of January&nbsp;31, 2003, we were in compliance with all of the covenants in our credit facilities, including those related to total debt and interest coverage, and under the most
restrictive covenant, we had the ability to issue additional debt of approximately $273.1&nbsp;million. Our borrowing capacity under these covenants can fluctuate substantially from quarter to
quarter depending upon our operating cash flow. Our borrowing capacity is typically lower in our fourth quarter, when operating results are also lower than in the other three quarters. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Convertible Senior Debentures  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On March&nbsp;21, 2003, we issued $350&nbsp;million principal amount of floating-rate convertible senior debentures due 2033 ("convertible
debentures"). An additional $50&nbsp;million principal amount of the convertible debentures were issued on April&nbsp;2, 2003, pursuant to an option granted to the initial purchasers. The
convertible debentures bear interest at a floating rate equal to 3-month LIBOR (reset quarterly) plus 0.75%, subject to a maximum of 6.75%. The convertible debentures also provide for the
payment of contingent interest if the average market price of the convertible debentures reaches a certain threshold. The convertible debentures provide for an initial base conversion price of $57.30
per share, reflecting a conversion premium of 100% over Mandalay's closing stock price of $28.65 on March&nbsp;17, 2003. The proceeds from this issuance were used to repay borrowings under our
revolving credit facility. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Each
convertible debenture will be convertible into shares of Mandalay's common stock (i)&nbsp;during any calendar quarter beginning after June&nbsp;30, 2003, if the closing price of
Mandalay's common stock is more than 120% of the base conversion price for at least 20 of the last 30 trading days of the preceding calendar quarter; (ii)&nbsp;if a credit rating assigned to the
convertible securities falls below a specified level; (iii)&nbsp;if we take certain corporate actions; or (iv)&nbsp;if we call the convertible securities for redemption. If the convertible
debentures are converted, holders will receive 17.452 shares per convertible debenture, or an aggregate of 7.0&nbsp;million shares of Mandalay common stock. In addition, if at the time of conversion
the market price of Mandalay's common stock exceeds the then-applicable base conversion price, holders will receive up to an additional 14.2789 shares of Mandalay's common stock per
convertible debenture, as determined pursuant to a specified formula, or up to an additional 5.7&nbsp;million shares in the aggregate. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;We
may redeem all or some of the convertible debentures for cash at any time on or after March&nbsp;21, 2008, at their accreted principal amount plus accrued and unpaid interest, if
any, to, but not including, the redemption date. At the option of the holders, we may be required to repurchase the convertible securities on the 5<SUP>th</SUP>, 10<SUP>th</SUP>,
15<SUP>th</SUP>, 20<SUP>th</SUP> and 25<SUP>th</SUP> anniversaries of their issuance, at their accreted principal amount plus accrued and unpaid interest, if any, to, but not including, the
purchase date. We may choose to pay the purchase price in cash, shares of Mandalay common stock or any combination thereof. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2><B>Senior Subordinated Notes and Senior Notes  </B></FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On July&nbsp;24, 2000, the Company issued $500&nbsp;million principal amount of 10<SUP>1</SUP>/<SMALL>4</SMALL>% Senior Subordinated Notes due August&nbsp;2007. And on
August&nbsp;16, 2000, the Company issued $200&nbsp;million principal amount of 9<SUP>1</SUP>/<SMALL>2</SMALL>% Senior Notes due August&nbsp;2008. These notes are not subject to any sinking fund
requirements. The net proceeds from these issuances were used to repay a portion of the borrowings under our then-existing credit facility. </FONT></P>

<P><FONT SIZE=2>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On
December&nbsp;20, 2001, we issued $300&nbsp;million principal amount of 9<SUP>3</SUP>/<SMALL>8</SMALL>% Senior Subordinated Notes due February&nbsp;2010. These notes are not subject to any
sinking fund requirements. The net proceeds from this issuance were used to repay a portion of the borrowings under our credit facilities. </FONT></P>
